doc.title: Change in accounting principle — retrospective
doc.event: Fyn Co. switches from average-cost to FIFO. Under FIFO, cumulative pre-tax income through the beginning of this year would have been $40,000 higher (tax rate 20%). Prepare the entry to adopt the change.

variants.label: Restate the past; RE catches the cumulative effect

lines.label: restated to FIFO
lines.why: Beginning inventory restates to what FIFO would have made it — the balance-sheet side of every prior year's difference, accumulated.
lines.trap: A change in PRINCIPLE is retrospective: prior statements presented are restated as if FIFO had always been used.

lines.label: cumulative effect, net of tax
lines.why: All that extra prior income lives in beginning RE now — 40,000 × (1 − 20%).
lines.trap: Never through current income. The 'cumulative effect on the income statement' treatment died decades ago; exams still bait with it.

lines.label: the tax on it
lines.why: Higher book inventory than tax basis → a temporary difference: 40,000 × 20%.
lines.trap: The cumulative effect is always net-of-tax; forgetting the deferred piece leaves the entry unbalanced or RE overstated.

memorize.body: Change in PRINCIPLE → retrospective: restate priors, adjust beginning RE for the cumulative effect net of tax, adjust the affected asset/liability, disclose.
Exception: a change TO LIFO (or any change where prior effects are impracticable to compute) is applied prospectively — no restatement, disclosure only.

questions.prompt: Adopting FIFO raises cumulative prior pre-tax income by 40,000 (tax 20%). Beginning Retained Earnings is adjusted by…

distractors.feedback: 40,000 is pre-tax. The cumulative-effect adjustment to RE is always NET of tax: 40,000 × 80% = 32,000, with the 8,000 to a deferred tax liability.

build.scaffold: Three lines: the asset restates, RE takes the after-tax cumulative effect, deferred tax takes the rest. The income-statement 'cumulative effect' account is a museum piece — pure bait.
